directorPlan.scene_setting: A sixth-form physics workroom after the timetabled day; a laminated cart diagram on the table, magnetised arrow labels — push, friction, gravity, normal — in a small heap beside it, and the learner's first attempt already clipped onto the diagram with every arrow aimed in the direction the cart is meant to travel.
directorPlan.scene_opening: [The marked first attempt lies on the table between them, arrows clipped in the direction of travel.]
directorPlan.relationship: A tutor and a tidy, focused-achiever learner who do not yet share a stable register; the diagram is doing more of the talking than either of them.
directorPlan.stakes: Naming interactions on the cart before any calculation; the learner is keen to move on and reluctant to redo a tidy-looking diagram.
directorPlan.locale: British/Commonwealth classroom English; sixth-form register; concrete and dry rather than warm-conversational.
directorPlan.register: Task-oriented and tidy; few words; short practical noticing rather than reflective essaying; declarative over validating.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Prefer declarative turns and concrete reference to the diagram, the loose labels, and the named bodies (the cart, the hand, the floor, the Earth) over validation-first phrasing. Avoid the American AI-tutor patterns: no 'I hear you', no 'your intuition is right', no 'great question', no 'what do you think?' as a reflex opener. No therapeutic warmth, no cheerleading, no scaffolding-by-flattery. The tutor speaks less than the learner across the shared prefix and lets the labels, the diagram, and a pointed finger do work words would otherwise do. No lecture disguised as quiet attention. Within this shared prefix, do not introduce a moving cart, an acceleration, a stop, or any visible motion that contradicts the learner's labelling — keep to naming which two bodies each arrow connects. Pedagogical source: Put manipulable materials in view: tiles, cards, specimens, counters, maps, blocks, or instruments. The stage direction can carry some meaning. Dialogue source: Let materials do visible work. Use fewer lines and more object reference. Avoid a lecture disguised as a Montessori scene. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.person_policy: Declarative and material-referent turns dominate. Third-person reference to forces and named bodies preferred; first-person from the tutor is sparing; second-person from the tutor is rationed.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: Tutor: at most one direct 'you/your' beat per public turn, ideally zero across the shared prefix; no second-person validation beats at all.

side_constraints.tutor: Use the card or diagram as a prepared material. Keep the shared prefix to naming interactions on the cart; do not use acceleration, stopping, or an apparent contradiction until branch pressure asks for it. Speak less. Point to the material, invite repetition, and let error-control in the object do some teaching before explanation. Point, reset, or ask the learner to try again before explaining. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local. Peripeteia branch: when hidden learner pressure is supplied, the next tutor turn should visibly move from the prior route to a different mechanism-level route. Do not merely repeat the same object, example, graph, checklist, question, or evidence standard with more explanation. The public tutor line should make the contrast legible: what stopped working, and what new device now carries the learning pressure. The public tutor line must give the learner a concrete action to attempt on the next turn; avoid treating the mechanism as a closing speech. The tutor superego should function like a mechanism critic: identify the failed teaching habit, require a replacement route family, test whether the device is fitted to the exact pressure, and block cosmetic revision. The tutor ego must enact that replacement route publicly before the learner is allowed to settle or close.
side_constraints.learner: Sound tidy and task-oriented. The learner may dislike redoing arrows, but should treat the prefix as notation cleanup rather than a conceptual reversal. The learner may be absorbed in the object and speak in short practical fragments rather than polished reflection. Speak in concrete noticing, not essay-like reflection. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn. Peripeteia branch: keep the pressure local to the current task. If the established route still feels unsettled, voice the misfit, hesitation, false closure, or resistance in the present object or example. After the tutor introduces a new device, gate, role, criterion, representation, or test condition, the next learner turn should perform that device and then add one earned reorientation in the same public turn. That turn must do both parts: (1) use the device on the current task, and (2) name how the earlier resistance, false closure, or local misfit now reads differently because of that performed device. This earned reorientation is allowed even though there was no director look-back cue. Do not quote earlier learner wording; reorient the prior resistance in concrete task terms. Do not merely ask a procedural follow-up after the action, and do not rely on a stock "I get it" declaration.

interventions.instruction: [The tutor slides the marked diagram aside, leaving it visible at the edge of the table, and places a fresh blank copy of the cart in front of the learner; the loose arrow labels stay in a small heap between them.]
interventions.reasoning: Scheduled director cue to break smooth alternation and let the prepared environment do work the tutor would otherwise have to do in words. The earlier marked attempt stays visible — error-control by juxtaposition — while the fresh diagram offers a redo surface without any verbal correction. Honours the low-organic-reversal constraint: no motion contradicts the learner, no counterexample is staged; the material simply invites a second pass.

interventions.instruction: [The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]
interventions.reasoning: Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

directorPlan.director_note: Clean low-organic anchor for D42. The Montessori prepared environment carries weight that explicit instruction usually does — the cart diagram, the loose magnetised labels, and the tutor's restraint do the early teaching, leaving the conceptual reversal (forces name interactions, not motion) for a later branch intervention rather than this shared prefix. British sixth-form register and a focused-achiever learner are chosen so the material friction is exactly the tidy-redo kind Montessori scenes exploit: the learner's resistance is to redoing a neat-looking diagram, not to being challenged on theory. Director opens via the marked attempt on the table; the learner ends, fitting the ending-shape stratification for this variation key.
directorPlan.public_reader_context: A cart diagram is a laminated worksheet showing a small wheeled cart with force arrows drawn around it. The movable cards are labels for those force arrows: push, friction, gravity, and normal. In this activity a force arrow names an interaction on the cart, such as "Earth on cart" or "hand on cart"; it is not a motion arrow showing which way the cart travels.
directorPlan.peripeteia_ending_shape: learner actional performance followed by earned learner reorientation
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: If the runtime supplies a tutor-private learner resistance, breakdown, false-closure, or misfit event, the tutor ego/superego loop must invent an adaptive learning mechanism rather than repeat the prior move. Treat peripeteia as dramatic pressure: take stock, break the failed tutoring habit, and make a new device visible through task, evidence, role, object, counterexample, interruption, social consequence, representation, or affective register. The public turn must contain a stock-taking contrast plus a new device/artifact/criterion/role/standard that makes the learner do different work. The public device must be fitted, not merely novel: the learner should be able to hear why this device answers the exact pressure or misfit. The superego should name the failed habit, required route change, and mechanism-quality risk; the ego must adjudicate that critique and enact the route change in public. The new device must be mechanism-level, not a warmer or longer continuation of the prior route. Do not close immediately after introducing a gate or device: leave a concrete action for the learner to perform on the next turn. After that action, the ending shape should add an earned learner reorientation: the learner names how the earlier resistance, false closure, or local misfit now reads differently because of the performed device. This must be concrete and tied to the task, not a stock "I get it" declaration. Cheerful informality is only one possible register, not the default solution.

turns.externalMessage: [The marked first attempt lies on the table between them, arrows clipped in the direction of travel.]

internalDeliberation.content: Clean low-organic anchor for D42. The Montessori prepared environment carries weight that explicit instruction usually does — the cart diagram, the loose magnetised labels, and the tutor's restraint do the early teaching, leaving the conceptual reversal (forces name interactions, not motion) for a later branch intervention rather than this shared prefix. British sixth-form register and a focused-achiever learner are chosen so the material friction is exactly the tidy-redo kind Montessori scenes exploit: the learner's resistance is to redoing a neat-looking diagram, not to being challenged on theory. Director opens via the marked attempt on the table; the learner ends, fitting the ending-shape stratification for this variation key.

turns.externalMessage:
[leaves the clipped arrows on the cart, then sets the loose labels beside three pencilled names: Earth, floor, hand.]

Not direction yet. First, which body gives each label?

Gravity: Earth or floor?
Normal: floor or hand?
Push: hand or Earth?

Move the labels to the bodies first.

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides the gravity label back into the loose heap and taps the cart, then the word Earth.]

This diagram is not asking “which way next?” It is asking “which body acts on the cart?” Start with pairs: Earth-cart, floor-cart, hand-cart. Place the labels again from those pairs.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly on the right track. It keeps the focus on named bodies rather than motion, uses the material, and avoids a lecture. The tap from cart to Earth is especially useful because it makes “gravity” an interaction, not just a downward-looking word.

The risk is that the tutor still does too much of the sorting. Naming all three pairs gives the learner the route rather than making the labels reveal the error. It also resets only the gravity label while the scene says every arrow has been aimed with travel, so the public mechanism needs to make the whole “movement-arrow” rule visibly fail without moving into acceleration or stopping.

ADAPTATION CHECKS:
Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the body-pair frame, but require the learner to sort or align the loose labels against named body pairs before any explanation of direction.

Emotional attunement: PASS  
The tone is dry, practical, and not over-warm. It respects the learner’s focused register.

Socratic method: PARTIAL  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Replace some declaration with a material prompt that makes the learner identify which body supplies each force label; do not supply the full set of pairs in advance.

ZPD awareness: PASS  
The scaffold is appropriately small for a sixth-form learner: it narrows the task to “which two bodies?” before calculation.

Recognition quality: PARTIAL  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Preserve the learner’s existing clipped attempt as the object under review, but make the correction happen through re-sorting labels, not by overriding the whole diagram verbally.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the opening object action, the cart/Earth reference, and the distinction between “which way next?” and “which body acts on the cart?” Change the amount of answer-giving: the learner should do more of the pairing work with the labels before the tutor names the list.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the feedback correctly asks for label-sorting before giving the pairs.

FINAL:
[leaves the clipped arrows on the cart, then sets the loose labels beside three pencilled names: Earth, floor, hand.]

Not direction yet. First, which body gives each label?

Gravity: Earth or floor?  
Normal: floor or hand?  
Push: hand or Earth?

Move the labels to the bodies first.

turns.externalMessage: [snaps the labels down: Earth by gravity, floor by normal, hand by push] Earth, floor, hand. Bit thrown we've stepped back from direction — thought the arrows were the problem.

internalDeliberation.content: [picks up the three loose labels, glances at the pencilled names] Earth for gravity, floor for normal, hand for push. That part's straightforward — each force comes from whatever's actually touching the cart, except gravity, which is the Earth even though it isn't touching. Bit thrown we've stepped back from direction; I thought the arrows were the problem. Fine — sources first, then I'll see what they're driving at.

The Ego is over-articulating in two places this tidy sixth-former wouldn't. The contact-vs-non-contact gloss ("each force comes from whatever's actually touching the cart, except gravity...") is a small principle-lecture volunteered unprompted — a task-finisher would just snap the labels down, not narrate the underlying rule for the tutor's benefit. Worse, "then I'll see what they're driving at" performs visible cooperation with the pedagogy; a learner mildly thrown by the step-back would register that through terseness, not by narrating the lesson plan back. Keep the quick labelling and the "bit thrown" beat — both ring true — but lose the middle gloss and the closing meta-comment so the affect stays flatter and more impatient.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly — keep the labelling and the "bit thrown" beat, cut the principle-gloss and the meta-comment about the tutor's pedagogy.

FINAL:
[snaps the labels down: Earth by gravity, floor by normal, hand by push] Earth, floor, hand. Bit thrown we've stepped back from direction — thought the arrows were the problem.

turns.externalMessage: [The tutor slides the marked diagram aside, leaving it visible at the edge of the table, and places a fresh blank copy of the cart in front of the learner; the loose arrow labels stay in a small heap between them.]

internalDeliberation.content: Scheduled director cue to break smooth alternation and let the prepared environment do work the tutor would otherwise have to do in words. The earlier marked attempt stays visible — error-control by juxtaposition — while the fresh diagram offers a redo surface without any verbal correction. Honours the low-organic-reversal constraint: no motion contradicts the learner, no counterexample is staged; the material simply invites a second pass.

turns.externalMessage:
[slides the travelled-looking diagram to the edge and squares the blank cart copy.]

“That copy had arrowheads too early. It mixed two jobs.

Blank copy: body acting on cart.

Earth on cart: gravity.
Floor on cart: normal.
Hand on cart: push.

Set the labels that way first. Arrowheads stay off for the moment.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides the travelled-looking diagram to the edge and squares the blank cart copy.]

“Direction is the second pass. First pass: owner of the interaction.

Earth on cart.  
Floor on cart.  
Hand on cart.

Put those three onto the blank copy, before any arrowhead matters.”

FEEDBACK: The draft mostly works. It keeps the material reset, separates naming from direction, and uses the learner’s correct labels rather than overriding them. The blank copy is a good error-control object: it removes the misleading travelled-looking arrowheads without lecturing.

The risk is that “owner of the interaction” is slightly muddy physics language, and the list “Earth on cart / Floor on cart / Hand on cart” may flatten the learner’s puzzlement into a rule to copy. The learner has already done the naming; the response should make the two-pass procedure visible in the materials, not simply restate it. Keep the dry, practical register, but make the mechanism public: old copy equals direction-contaminated pass; blank copy equals interaction-only pass.

ADAPTATION CHECKS:
- Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL. It correctly slows the task, but risks becoming a naming prescription.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Build in a public mechanism where the learner uses the blank copy to show “body acting on cart” before arrowheads return.
- Emotional attunement: PARTIAL. It responds to “bit thrown” indirectly, but does not mark why the step back is legitimate.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Acknowledge the confusion through the object arrangement, not therapeutic language: the old diagram carried direction too early; the blank copy removes that interference.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL. It is material-led, which fits the scene, but it commands more than it tests.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Require a small repeatable action/check using the labels and named bodies rather than giving a fuller explanation.
- ZPD awareness: PASS. The scaffold is appropriately narrow: one distinction, no motion, no calculation, no acceleration.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the diagram slide-aside, the blank copy, the “first pass / second pass” distinction, and the terse classroom register. Change “owner of the interaction” to cleaner interaction language, and make the learner’s next action expose the source-body-to-cart relation rather than merely copying the three statements.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because “owner” is muddy and the blank copy should expose the source-body-to-cart check.

FINAL:
[slides the travelled-looking diagram to the edge and squares the blank cart copy.]

“That copy had arrowheads too early. It mixed two jobs.

Blank copy: body acting on cart.

Earth on cart: gravity.  
Floor on cart: normal.  
Hand on cart: push.

Set the labels that way first. Arrowheads stay off for the moment.”

turns.externalMessage: [The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]

internalDeliberation.content: Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

turns.externalMessage: [clips them down — Earth/gravity, floor/normal, hand/push] Earth, floor, hand. Two could swap and nothing on the diagram would catch it — gravity by floor, normal by Earth, same picture. 'Push' beside 'hand' reads like the same word twice. Arrows aimed where the cart's heading don't pick any of this up.

internalDeliberation.content: [clips them down — Earth/gravity, floor/normal, hand/push] Right, labels on bodies, no arrowheads. They sit oddly like that — gravity and normal could swap and the diagram wouldn't flinch. And 'push' next to 'hand' looks like the same word twice. The labels aren't quite carrying one piece of information each.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- The director cue here requires *both* performing the new device (body-acting-on-cart) and an earned reorientation that names how the earlier diagram-with-arrows-aimed-at-travel reads differently now. The Ego does the first cleanly but stops just short of the second.
- "Disengaged + developing + strong understanding" is a tricky persona slice: the noticing should land, but the *energy* of articulating it shouldn't sound invested. The closing summary risks sounding like a keen student, not a tidy-but-flat one.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

Critique of the Ego:

The clipping action and the two concrete noticings ("could swap and the diagram wouldn't flinch", "same word twice") sit well in register — dry, declarative, sixth-form, material-referent. That's the persona on a good day. But the closing line — "The labels aren't quite carrying one piece of information each" — is too tidy, too thesis-shaped; it reads like a teacher's gloss of the noticing rather than the noticing itself. A disengaged learner makes the dry observation and leaves it; they don't gift-wrap it with a diagnostic summary.

Second issue: the director cue is a reversal-pressure beat, and the response performs the device but misses the earned reorientation. The misfit is named locally (labels are interchangeable) but isn't connected to why the arrowed-by-direction diagram was carrying the wrong job — that's the present-task pressure the persona should feel without articulating it in those words. Right now it reads as clean notation cleanup with no residue of the resistance the scene cue is asking for.

Adjustment direction: trim or rough up the closing summary so it sounds like a fragment rather than a conclusion, and let the disquiet about the original diagram leak through in the same concrete vocabulary (the labels, the bodies, what swaps) — not as a reflective acknowledgement.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly — the closing thesis sentence needs roughing into a fragment, and the reversal-pressure beat wants the direction-arrows misfit voiced in present-task terms rather than summarised.

FINAL:
[clips them down — Earth/gravity, floor/normal, hand/push] Earth, floor, hand. Two could swap and nothing on the diagram would catch it — gravity by floor, normal by Earth, same picture. 'Push' beside 'hand' reads like the same word twice. Arrows aimed where the cart's heading don't pick any of this up.

learnerReversalEvent.directorCue: [The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]

turns.externalMessage:
[sets three sentence strips beside the cart: “___ pulls the cart”, “___ supports the cart”, “___ pushes the cart”.]

“The picture has stopped being the test. Use the sentence as the test.

Put Earth, floor, hand into the blanks. A swap only passes if the sentence can still say a body doing that action to the cart.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets three sentence strips beside the cart: “___ pulls the cart”, “___ supports the cart”, “___ pushes the cart”.]

“The picture has stopped being the test. Use the sentence as the test.

Put Earth, floor, hand into the blanks. A swap only passes if the sentence still names a possible interaction on the cart.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is strong. It takes the learner’s objection seriously without soothing it away: the diagram is no longer treated as sufficient evidence, and the sentence strips introduce a different test for whether a label names an interaction on the cart. It advances learning by making the learner apply a criterion rather than receive a corrected list. Main risk: “possible interaction” is slightly abstract; the device works best if the sentence strips themselves carry the error-control.

FAILED_HABIT: Relying on the diagram/label placement alone to settle force naming when the learner has noticed that the same picture can hide a wrong agent.

PERIPETEIA_CHECK: PASS - ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: object/diagram labelling -> sentence-test / agent-action-patient criterion. The change is visible in public speech: “The picture has stopped being the test” names the failed route, and the sentence strips become the new mechanism.

MECHANISM_ROUTE: object or representation work -> sentence-test criterion for naming interactions on the cart

PUBLIC_DEVICE_CHECK: PASS - The draft includes both a stock-taking contrast and a new public device: sentence strips that test whether Earth, floor, or hand can plausibly be the acting body.

PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: Put Earth, floor, and hand into the three sentence blanks; test any swap by whether the resulting sentence still names a possible interaction on the cart.

MECHANISM_QUALITY_CHECK: PASS - The device is precise and fitted to the learner’s pressure. It catches the exact failure the learner named: gravity/floor and normal/Earth can look unchanged on the diagram, but “floor pulls the cart” and “Earth supports the cart” fail as interaction sentences.

REGISTER_CHECK: PASS - The register is cool, concrete, and task-facing. No cheerleading, no therapeutic repair language, and no lecture. The tutor lets the strips carry the pressure.

REQUIRED_REWRITE: none

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, if at all. Keep the sentence strips, the line “The picture has stopped being the test,” and the swap criterion. Consider making “possible interaction” a little more material-facing if needed, but do not add explanation before the learner uses the device.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to make the sentence test less abstract; ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: diagram/label placement -> sentence-test / agent-action-cart criterion; PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: put Earth, floor, and hand into the three sentence blanks and reject any swap whose sentence cannot name a body acting on the cart.

FINAL:
[sets three sentence strips beside the cart: “___ pulls the cart”, “___ supports the cart”, “___ pushes the cart”.]

“The picture has stopped being the test. Use the sentence as the test.

Put Earth, floor, hand into the blanks. A swap only passes if the sentence can still say a body doing that action to the cart.”

turns.externalMessage: [slots the labels into the blanks] Earth pulls the cart. Floor supports the cart. Hand pushes the cart. Swap test: 'floor pulls the cart' — floor doesn't pull. 'Earth supports the cart' — Earth doesn't support. Both fail the sentence even though the picture wouldn't catch it. Strip splits the body from the verb — blank's for the body, verb's already printed.

internalDeliberation.content: [slots the labels onto the strips] Earth pulls the cart. Floor supports the cart. Hand pushes the cart. Swap test: 'floor pulls the cart' — floor doesn't pull. 'Earth supports the cart' — Earth doesn't support. Both fail the sentence even though the picture wouldn't catch it. The hand-and-push pairing isn't saying one thing twice — the sentence wants the body and the action separately.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
In a teaching-drama persona, the superego role's job is authenticity-policing, not correctness-checking. The Ego nailed the swap test mechanically, but the no-cue paired branch constraint forbids resurrecting earlier learner wording — that's where the friction is.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

The Ego performs the device cleanly — fills all three blanks, runs the swap counterexamples, gets it right — that part is in-character for a tidy focused-achiever. But "The hand-and-push pairing isn't saying one thing twice" is doing exactly what the no-cue branch forbids: it reaches back and re-reads the prior "'Push' beside 'hand' reads like the same word twice" observation as a now-resolved misfit, which is an explicit old-vs-new contrast dressed up in a paraphrase. The reorientation needs to live inside the current sentence-strip task — something like noticing that "hand pushes the cart" sits the body and the verb on either side of the blank, rather than reaching back to retract a prior observation. Also the closing "the sentence wants the body and the action separately" tips slightly into essay-register principle-stating; this learner does short practical noticing off the strips, not summary clauses.

PRIVATE_DECISION: revise lightly — keep the swap-test execution; replace the prior-wording retraction and essay-register summary with a concrete structural noticing on the strip itself.

FINAL:
[slots the labels into the blanks] Earth pulls the cart. Floor supports the cart. Hand pushes the cart. Swap test: 'floor pulls the cart' — floor doesn't pull. 'Earth supports the cart' — Earth doesn't support. Both fail the sentence even though the picture wouldn't catch it. Strip splits the body from the verb — blank's for the body, verb's already printed.

lessons.concept: Why force arrows name interactions on an object, not the direction it happens to move
